planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost or in fall; in zone 9 sow October–February for winter-spring bloom. Resina calendula is best grown as a cool-season annual in zone 9 — it flowers prolifically in cooler months (November–April) and declines in summer heat. The sticky, resinous flowers should be harvested with gloves as the resin stains hands and fabric. Harvest flowers in the morning when fully open.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; Resina is a calendula cultivar specifically selected by the Heritage Seed Program for maximum resin content rather than ornamental appeal — the flower heads are visibly more resinous and sticky than standard ornamental calendula varieties, with a higher concentration of the triterpenoid saponins and flavonoids that make calendula one of the most important herbs in traditional and contemporary herbal medicine. The high-resin flowers are the preferred type for making calendula-infused oil (used in salves, lip balms, and skin care products), tinctures, and medicinal teas where the active compound concentration per flower is significantly higher than in standard varieties. Resina flowers are single to semi-double in pale to deep orange and yellow, with the characteristic sticky feel of the bracts when harvested. In zone 9 the cool-season planting from October through February produces the most productive and resinous flower crop.
sources: Heritage Seed Program — Resina Calendula Selection Notes
University of Vermont Extension — Medicinal Herb Production
American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Calendula officinalis Monograph

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface (seeds need light to germinate) 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 sow October–January for winter-spring harvest. Do not cover seeds with soil — press lightly into the surface and keep moist. Bodegold self-sows abundantly; allow some seedlings to naturalize for a self-perpetuating chamomile patch. Harvest flowers just as the petals begin to reflex downward for highest essential oil content.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, poor-to-moderate soil — chamomile prefers lean soil and self-seeds most productively when not over-fertilized; Bodegold is a German-selected high-yield commercial chamomile cultivar developed for maximum essential oil production — the flowers contain 2–3 times the essential oil of wild-type chamomile plants, with particularly high chamazulene (the anti-inflammatory blue compound responsible for chamomile oil's characteristic deep blue color when steam-distilled). This variety is widely grown in Germany, Hungary, and Eastern Europe for pharmaceutical and cosmetic chamomile production where standardized high-oil content is required. For home herbal use, Bodegold provides substantially stronger tea and extract from the same weight of dried flowers compared to ordinary chamomile. The apple-scented white flowers with yellow centers are used fresh or dried for chamomile tea, herbal baths, hair rinses, and digestive tinctures.
sources: German Federal Institute for Risk Assessment — Medicinal Chamomile Varieties
Herb Research Foundation — High-Oil Chamomile Cultivars
University of Vermont Extension — Matricaria chamomilla Commercial Production

planting.note: Direct sow around last frost date when soil has warmed to 55°F; in zone 9 sow September–February for fall-through-spring bloom. Whirlybird is compact and well-suited to containers, hanging baskets, and small garden spaces. Soak large seeds overnight before planting for faster germination. In zone 9 nasturtiums thrive in the cool season and decline with summer heat — let them reseed for fall germination.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, poor-to-moderate soil — nasturtiums bloom most profusely in lean soil; Whirlybird is an All-America Selections Award winner distinguished from standard trailing nasturtiums by its compact mound habit and spurless flowers that face upward (toward the viewer) above the foliage rather than hiding below the leaves like older varieties. The upward-facing semi-double flowers in a mix of cherry, tangerine, gold, mahogany, and cream shades make Whirlybird the most impressive nasturtium for cut flower use and garden display — the flowers are fully visible from above rather than requiring you to lift the leaves to appreciate them. The spurless character (no nectar spur) also means the flowers are technically easier to use as edible garnishes where the spur's somewhat bitter flavor is eliminated. Like all nasturtiums, the flowers, leaves, and seeds are edible — the peppery, watercress-like flavor of the leaves and the bright, slightly sweet-peppery flowers are popular in salads, compound butters, and as garnishes.
sources: Cornell University Extension — Edible Nasturtium Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cool-Season Edible Flowers

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–May for summer harvest. Red Kuri stores remarkably well — properly cured fruits (2 weeks at 85°F, then cool dry storage) keep 5–6 months, providing winter squash into late winter. The medium-size fruits (5–8 lbs) are manageable for home cooking. In zone 9 the long, warm season allows the fruit to cure fully on the vine — leave until the vine withers and the skin is hard before harvesting.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Red Kuri (also known as Red Hokkaido, Orange Hokkaido, or Uchiki Kuri) is a Japanese heirloom winter squash producing distinctive, medium-sized, pear-to-teardrop-shaped fruits with deep reddish-orange skin and bright orange, dry, starchy flesh. The flavor is described as a rich chestnut-like sweetness with buttery, nutty notes and an exceptionally dense, dry texture that distinguishes it from the moister, stringier flesh of butternut and acorn squash. The dry, starchy texture makes Red Kuri outstanding for soups and purees (where it thickens naturally), Japanese-style simmered preparations (kabocha), and stuffed squash presentations where watery flesh would dilute the flavor. The medium size (5–8 pounds) is more practical for home cooking than very large winter squash. Red Kuri has been popular in French and Japanese cuisine for decades and is now widely grown in American specialty gardens and farmers markets.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Winter Squash
Cornell University Extension — Cucurbita maxima Heirloom Varieties
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Red Kuri History and Production

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; in zone 9 plant March–June. Musquee de Provence requires a long, warm growing season — at least 110 days of warm weather. In zone 9 planting in April gives the fruit time to cure fully on the vine. As a Cucurbita moschata, it has better heat and humidity tolerance than Cucurbita maxima types. The large fruits (12–20+ lbs) benefit from being elevated on boards or straw to prevent rot where soil stays moist.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, rich, slightly acidic soil; Musquee de Provence (Musk of Provence) is a magnificent French heirloom pumpkin from the Provence region, producing large, deeply ridged, flat-round fruits with a muted tan to bronze-brown skin that develops rich russet-copper tones when fully mature. The deep orange-yellow flesh is exceptionally sweet, dense, and aromatic — the 'musquee' (musky) name refers to the intensely sweet, rich, slightly musky fragrance and flavor of the ripe flesh, considered among the finest-flavored of any large pumpkin. Fully matured fruits from this Cucurbita moschata variety develop complex flavor notes beyond ordinary pumpkin sweetness. The large, photogenic fruits (often 12–20 pounds) are classic French market pumpkins seen in Provençal farmers' markets in fall. In zone 9 the summer heat and humidity are well tolerated by this C. moschata type, which is more disease-resistant in warm climates than C. maxima varieties.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Musquee de Provence Pumpkin
Seed Savers Exchange — French Heirloom Pumpkin Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Winter Squash and Pumpkin Production
